review_summary: This unit supports focused questions on the attract-repel structure of contrastive learning and the InfoNCE connection to softmax classification and mutual information.

question_bank.question: In contrastive learning, what are positive and negative samples?
choices: Positives are related views of the same object; negatives come from different objects.
Positives are manually labeled categories; negatives are unlabeled examples.
Positives are high-resolution images; negatives are low-resolution images.
Positives are decoder outputs; negatives are encoder inputs.
question_bank.explanation: Contrastive learning defines relationships between examples. Views of the same underlying item should be close, while views from different items should be separated.

question_bank.question: How does the contrastive softmax objective resemble multiclass cross-entropy?
choices: It treats the positive pair as the correct class among many negative alternatives.
It reconstructs every input pixel with mean squared error.
It uses only one example and no denominator term.
It requires a human label for every negative sample.
question_bank.explanation: The model scores one positive and many negatives, then uses a softmax-style objective to make the positive score dominate. This mirrors selecting the correct class in multiclass classification.

question_bank.question: Why do many InfoNCE-style contrastive methods benefit from many negative samples?
choices: More negatives tighten the mutual-information lower bound and provide stronger contrastive comparisons.
More negatives remove the need for positive pairs.
More negatives make the softmax denominator disappear.
More negatives guarantee perfect downstream accuracy without training.
question_bank.explanation: InfoNCE is motivated as a lower bound on mutual information between positive pairs. More negative examples make that bound tighter and give the model richer comparisons.
